one_line_takeaway: A NeurIPS position paper proposing that the conference require a tiered (public/controlled/restricted) disclosure and claim-inventory system for frontier AI safety claims, arguing that non-reproducibility of the most consequential safety assertions should be treated as a peer-review methodology failure rather than an acceptable transparency preference; it is a review-policy proposal, not a security study.
key_finding: The paper's central (argued, not empirically derived by the authors) claim is that transparency and reproducibility for frontier AI safety claims have been declining even as those claims become more consequential for governance -- termed the 'evidential inversion.' The proposed remedy is procedural: NeurIPS should adopt a claim inventory, mandatory scope statements, and a three-tier disclosure system (public / confidential-panel-reviewed / restricted-and-right-scaled) with a phased rollout (checklist in Year 1, a capped five-paper confidential-panel pilot in Year 2, disclosure labels and graduated sanctions in Year 3), rather than treating non-reproducibility of safety claims as an acceptable transparency preference.
core_contribution: A NeurIPS position paper (Vishwarupe, Flechais, Jirotka, Shadbolt; Oxford) arguing that NeurIPS should require reproducibility standards for 'frontier AI safety claims' (published assertions that a highly capable general-purpose model is below a threshold of concern, adequately mitigated, or suitable for release). The paper's own contributions are: (1) an operational definition of a frontier AI safety claim, anchored either to a compute threshold (EU AI Act's 10^25 FLOPs or California SB-53's 10^26 FLOPs) or to evidence of emergent dangerous capability (CBRN uplift, autonomous cyber-offence, self-replication, strategic deception), plus a normative/descriptive scoping rule; (2) a diagnosis they term the 'evidential inversion' -- the claim that the most consequential AI safety assertions are the least reproducible; (3) a three-tier disclosure framework (T1 Public, T2 Controlled via a 'federated colloquium' of secure-review hosts such as national AI Safety Institutes, T3 Restricted/right-scaled) paired with a mandatory claim inventory and scope statements; (4) five proposed additions to the NeurIPS author checklist (S1-S5); (5) a phased implementation path (Phase 1 checklist, Phase 2 five-paper confidential-panel pilot, Phase 3 disclosure labels and graduated sanctions); and (6) responses to five anticipated objections. This is a policy/institutional-design proposal for conference peer review, not an empirical study, attack, defense, or measurement of any AI system.
threat_model: None. The paper does not propose or analyze an attacker, an attack surface, or a defense mechanism against adversarial behavior. It is a research-governance/reproducibility-policy proposal aimed at conference reviewers, program chairs, and paper authors. Where it discusses 'risk' it means the risk that safety claims made by AI developers are unverifiable or overstated (an epistemic/evaluation-validity concern), not a security threat model in the adversary-vs-defender sense.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations (Section 8): the proposal is 'a phased scientific-review standard, not a ready-made regulatory infrastructure'; Tier 2 controlled review is capacity-constrained and should begin only as a bounded pilot (explicitly capped at five papers per review cycle) after the Year-1 checklist baseline has been tested in practice. The paper also self-identifies several unresolved pitfalls it does not fully solve: confidential-panel capture risk (mitigated only by term limits and recusal rules, not eliminated), threshold-anchor obsolescence as compute efficiency improves, and the practical burden of building secure T2 review infrastructure at conference scale. Because this is a position/proposal paper, none of its mechanisms (claim inventory, tiering, panel) have been piloted or validated empirically at the time of writing; the worked T2 example in Appendix B is explicitly synthetic/hypothetical, not a real case.

quantitative_results: 2025 Foundation Model Transparency Index: sector-average transparency score of 40/100, down 9 points year-over-year for weighted-average flagship developers (cited from Wan et al. 2025, not produced by this paper)
Six major developers (including OpenAI, Google, and xAI) scored zero on model-information disclosures, and no developer adequately reported train-test overlap (cited from Wan et al. 2025)
Twelve leading AI companies published or updated Frontier AI Safety Frameworks in 2025 (cited from Bengio et al. 2026)
Phase 2 pilot bounded to at most five papers per review cycle, with a 5-7 member confidential panel
Estimated cost of the Phase 2 pilot: 200-300 panel-hours (40-60 hours per submission across 5-7 members)

Illustrative (synthetic, not real) Appendix B example: hypothetical N=2,400-prompt CBRN red-team set; hypothetical refusal-rate increase from 71% to 94% (delta = 23 percentage points, 95% CI [19.4, 26.7]); hypothetical judge-model disagreement of ~8% with majority human adjudication on a 200-prompt calibration sample -- these are fabricated illustrative numbers explicitly labeled as synthetic by the authors, not empirical results
